title: Choosing a first pyrography kit
subtitle: What to buy, what to avoid, and what to practise on first
excerpt: A guide to choosing the right pyrography kit for a first project: the difference between solid-tip and nichrome-wire burners, which tips are essential, what wood and board to start on, and what the first practice session should cover. Covers the two most common equipment mistakes and how to avoid them.
sourceNotes: Victorian pokerwork manuals for introductory equipment guidance; contemporary craft practice for modern equipment comparison.

content.text: . The solid-tip is the cheaper option and is widely sold in craft kits; the nichrome-wire unit is the tool that professional and serious amateur pyrographers use. The difference in control is significant.

A solid-tip burner heats the tip slowly and cools it slowly. It operates at a fixed or coarsely adjustable temperature. Changing tips takes several minutes of cooling. For geometric work, lettering, and simple decorative outlines, the solid-tip is adequate. For tonal shading or fine line work on animals or portraits, the slow temperature response limits what is possible. The tip also tends to drag at lower temperatures rather than burning cleanly.

A nichrome-wire burner changes temperature in seconds when the dial is adjusted. Tips swap in under a minute when cool. The fine wire tips produce a sharper mark than the broad solid brass tips of the entry unit. For a maker who intends to continue past a first project, the nichrome-wire burner is worth the higher starting cost. The essential tips to begin with are a writing tip, a shading tip, and a ball stylus.

Birch plywood is the best starting surface: the pale, even grain burns to a consistent tone and the smooth face requires no surface preparation beyond 240-grit sanding. Avoid resinous woods such as pine for first projects; the resin pockets cause irregular dark spots that are unrelated to tip pressure. Similarly, avoid reclaimed or treated timber; the heat can release unpleasant fumes from preservatives.

On a scrap of birch, practise three things before you start a finished piece. First, a straight continuous line at a slow pace. Second, the same line at a faster pace (note the difference in tone). Third, a series of short tapered strokes lifted at the end of each movement. These three stroke types cover ninety per cent of all pyrography work. A cooling stand on the bench is not optional. Return the burner to the stand between every stroke. That habit must be automatic before you start any project.

items.fix: Allow the burner to reach full working temperature before the first stroke; this takes 2 to 3 minutes for a solid-tip burner and about 30 seconds for a nichrome-wire unit. The tip is ready when a very light touch to a scrap piece leaves a clear pale line immediately.
items.cause: Tip not at full working temperature before touching the wood, or temperature dial set too low
items.symptom: Tip drags across the wood surface without burning cleanly

items.fix: Compensate by increasing tip pressure or slowing the stroke when crossing the grain. On birch plywood the grain variation is minor; on solid timber it is more pronounced. Always test on a scrap piece cut from the same blank.
items.cause: Wood grain: lines burned with the grain are lighter than lines burned across the grain at the same temperature
items.symptom: Marks are inconsistent in darkness even at a steady speed
